_disclaimer: State-level policy summary — for reference and UI demonstration. Verify with current legislation before reliance.

04.name: Arizona
04.summary: Transaction privilege tax exemption for qualifying data centers. Major hyperscale destination.

sources.label: Arizona Department of Revenue — Data Center Exemptions

06.name: California
06.summary: Multiple AI transparency laws enacted: AB 2013 (AI training disclosure), AB 2885 (AI definitions), AB 1008 (privacy). SB 1047 (high-risk AI) vetoed by Gov. Newsom in Sep 2024. CCPA/CPRA data privacy rules apply broadly to AI systems.

sources.label: California Legislative Information — SB 1047 veto
AB 2013 — AI Training Data Transparency Act

08.name: Colorado
08.summary: SB 205 (2024) — Colorado Artificial Intelligence Act: nation's first comprehensive state AI regulation. Requires risk assessments and impact notices for developers and deployers of high-risk AI systems. Signed by Gov. Polis May 2024.

sources.label: Colorado SB 205 — Artificial Intelligence Act (2024)

09.name: Connecticut
09.summary: Multiple AI regulation bills proposed 2024–2025 session. Connecticut Data Privacy Act (CTDPA) applies to AI-driven automated decision-making.

sources.label: Connecticut General Assembly

13.name: Georgia
13.summary: Sales and use tax exemption on data center equipment and energy. Major growth market for Google, Microsoft, and NCR.

sources.label: Georgia Department of Revenue — Technology Tax Exemptions

17.name: Illinois
17.summary: Artificial Intelligence Video Interview Act (2020): requires disclosure and consent before AI-analyzed video interviews. BIPA biometric data law covers AI facial recognition. Additional AI employment rules.

sources.label: IL AIVIA — 820 ILCS 42
IL Biometric Information Privacy Act (BIPA)

18.name: Indiana
18.summary: Indiana continues to offer data center tax exemptions on equipment and electricity for qualified facilities, and remains a major hyperscale hub (Meta, Microsoft, Amazon). However, as of mid-2026, nearly a third of Indiana's 92 counties have enacted data center restrictions, representing one of the most extensive statewide county-level moratorium waves in the US. Indiana University's Environmental Resilience Institute identified at least 17 counties with temporary moratoriums and two — Marshall County and Cass County — that have enacted permanent bans. Additional counties with active moratoriums include Boone, White, Putnam, Starke (Dec 2025), Dearborn (Feb 2026), Fulton (Mar 2026), Madison (Jun 2026), and others. An Indianapolis City-County Council committee voted 10-3 on July 13, 2026 to recommend a moratorium through December 31, 2027, with a full council vote scheduled for August 10, 2026. County concerns center on water resources, energy grid capacity, agricultural land preservation, and rural community character.

sources.label: Indiana Economic Development Corporation
WFYI: Nearly a third of Indiana counties have moved to restrict data centers (July 2026)
WFYI: Indianapolis data center moratorium committee vote (July 2026)

19.name: Iowa
19.summary: 0% sales and use tax on data center equipment and electricity for qualifying facilities (Iowa Code §423.3). Considered the most aggressive state data center incentive package in the US. Google, Microsoft, Facebook all have large campuses.

sources.label: Iowa Code § 423.3 — Data Center Exemptions

25.name: Massachusetts
25.summary: Multiple AI oversight bills proposed in 2024–2025 sessions. No comprehensive AI law enacted as of 2025. Consumer protection laws applied to AI systems by AG.

sources.label: Massachusetts General Court

31.name: Nebraska
31.summary: LB 1043 data center investment incentive program. Tax credits for qualifying large-scale data center projects. Low energy costs.

sources.label: Nebraska Legislature — LB 1043

32.name: Nevada
32.summary: Personal property tax abatements and sales tax exemptions for qualifying data centers. Switch Tahoe Reno Industrial Center and other major operators benefit from state incentives.

sources.label: Nevada Governor's Office of Economic Development

36.name: New York
36.summary: New York became the first US state to enact a statewide data center moratorium. Governor Kathy Hochul signed Executive Order No. 62 on July 14, 2026, pausing discretionary New York State Department of Environmental Conservation (DEC) permitting for new hyperscale data centers at 50 megawatts (MW) and above for up to one year. The order also requires a Generic Environmental Impact Statement (GEIS) assessing data center energy demand, water use, and air quality, and provides guidance for local entities to negotiate community benefits within 60 days. The NY State Legislature had separately passed the Responsible Data Center Development Act (S10642/A11560) on June 4, 2026 (20 MW threshold, additional mandates) which Hochul declined to sign, choosing to act via executive order instead. Local context: NYC Local Law 144 (2023) requires bias audits for automated employment tools; the Lysander Town Board (Onondaga County) also enacted a 6-month local moratorium on May 7, 2026.

sources.label: NY Governor Hochul — Executive Order No. 62 (official)
NY Governor Hochul — First Statewide Moratorium on Hyperscale Data Centers (press release)
NY Senate Bill S10642 — Responsible Data Center Development Act
NYC Local Law 144 — Automated Employment Decision Tools

37.name: North Carolina
37.summary: Data center investment incentives. Sales and property tax exemptions for qualifying facilities. Apple (Maiden), Google (Lenoir), and other hyperscalers operate major campuses.

sources.label: NC Department of Commerce — Incentives

39.name: Ohio
39.summary: Data center sales and use tax exemptions on equipment and energy (Ohio R.C. 5739.02). Major AWS, Meta, and Microsoft facilities in the Columbus metro area.

sources.label: Ohio Development — Data Center Incentives

41.name: Oregon
41.summary: Property tax exemptions for qualifying data centers in the Columbia River corridor. Google (The Dalles), Amazon, Apple have major campuses. Note: Hood River County has enacted a municipal data center ban (see county data).

sources.label: Business Oregon — Data Center Exemptions

45.name: South Carolina
45.summary: Data center sales tax exemption on equipment and electricity for qualifying investments. Amazon/AWS has major facilities in Berkeley County. State actively courts hyperscale investment.

sources.label: SCDOR — Manufacturing & Data Center Exemptions

27.name: Minnesota
27.summary: Minnesota enacted the Minnesota Consumer Data Privacy Act (MCDPA) in 2024, creating data privacy obligations for AI systems. Minneapolis (Hennepin County) has banned government facial recognition and requires AI impact assessments. Minneapolis-area municipalities have proposed data center moratoriums. Minnesota does not have a statewide data center tax incentive as of 2025.

sources.label: Minnesota Consumer Data Privacy Act (MCDPA, 2024)

sources: Minneapolis City Council facial recognition ban (2021)

47.name: Tennessee
47.summary: Sales tax exemption on data center equipment for qualifying facilities (Tenn. Code Ann. § 67-6-206). xAI Colossus supercomputer facility (world's largest GPU cluster as of 2024) operates in Memphis (Shelby County). Multiple TN counties enacted data center moratoriums in 2024 citing TVA grid capacity concerns.

sources.label: Tennessee Department of Revenue — Data Center Exemption
xAI Colossus Memphis

48.name: Texas
48.summary: No state income tax. Chapter 403 successor incentive programs for large capital projects. No state AI regulation enacted as of 2025. One of the largest US data center markets.

sources.label: Texas Economic Development — Incentives

51.name: Virginia
51.summary: Virginia remains the world's largest data center market (Northern Virginia handles 70%+ of global internet traffic) and the largest US state market. On June 30, 2026, Governor Abigail Spanberger signed the 2026 biennial budget (HB30) which includes a new Data Center Electricity Consumption Tax of $0.011 per kWh, effective July 1, 2026. The tax applies to both utility-supplied and self-generated electricity at facilities of 1 MW or more. Revenue is capped at $600 million per year, with refunds if collections exceed the cap. The tax expires June 30, 2028 absent legislative extension. The existing sales and use tax exemption for qualifying data center equipment was preserved. Individual counties have significant local restrictions — see county data for Loudoun, Fauquier, Page, and Rappahannock counties in particular.

sources.label: Virginia HB30 — Data Center Electricity Consumption Tax (budget amendment 3-5.24#1c)
Williams Mullen: Virginia Budget Creates New Electricity Consumption Tax for Data Centers
Data Center Knowledge: Virginia Approves First-Ever Data Center Power Tax
Virginia DHCD — Data Center Incentive Package

53.name: Washington
53.summary: Governor's Executive Order 23-03 on AI (2023). SHB 1951 (2024): requires state agencies to inventory AI systems. Multiple additional AI governance bills enacted. Growing data center market in the Columbia Basin.

sources.label: WA Governor's Executive Order EO-23-03
WA SHB 1951 — AI Accountability in Government (2024)

22.name: Louisiana
22.summary: Industrial Tax Exemption Program (ITEP) provides up to 100% property tax exemptions for 10 years for qualifying industrial facilities including data centers. No state income tax on manufacturing equipment. Meta announced a major data center in Richland Parish ($800M+) in 2023.

sources.label: Louisiana Economic Development — ITEP Program
Meta Richland Parish Louisiana Data Center

26.name: Michigan
26.summary: Michigan has proposed AI governance legislation in 2024–2025 sessions including data privacy bills. Several counties (Kalamazoo, Washtenaw) have proposed data center moratoriums as the state weighs large-scale data center incentive packages. Michigan does not have a statewide data center tax exemption as of 2025.

sources.label: Michigan Legislature — AI and Data Privacy Bills

44.name: Rhode Island
44.summary: Rhode Island has no statewide data center incentive program. The Town of Smithfield (Providence County) enacted a permanent ban on large data centers in 2023 — one of the first US municipalities to do so — citing energy grid, water, and community impacts. This ban has been cited as a model by municipalities in other states.

sources.label: Smithfield RI data center ban — Data Center Bans

55.name: Wisconsin
55.summary: Wisconsin offers data center property tax exemptions for qualifying facilities. Microsoft announced a $3.3B, 1GW+ AI data center campus in Mount Pleasant (Racine County) in May 2024. However, multiple Wisconsin counties and municipalities have enacted data center moratoriums as of 2026. Dane County (Madison) enacted an 18-month moratorium on hyperscale data centers (≥5,000 servers, ≥10,000 sq ft) in unincorporated county areas, signed by County Executive Melissa Agard after a unanimous board vote on June 4-5, 2026. The City of Madison enacted a separate moratorium on January 13, 2026. Manitowoc County has proposed data center restrictions. Additional moratoriums have been enacted in the town of Cassville, Westport (Dane County), Baldwin (St. Croix County), and Door County. See county-level data for specific details.

sources.label: Wisconsin Economic Development Corporation
Microsoft Mount Pleasant Wisconsin AI Campus
Dane County — Board introduces moratorium on data centers (official press release)
City of Madison — Temporary Data Center Moratorium

56.name: Wyoming
56.summary: Low energy costs, no state income tax, minimal regulation. Growing destination for crypto mining and data centers. Supports high-performance computing.

sources.label: Wyoming Business Council

12.ai_policy: Florida AI in Government Act (SB 1680, 2023) — establishes AI use guidelines for state agencies
12.notes: Florida actively courts data center investment. HB 7063 provides 20-year sales tax exemptions.
12.name: Florida
12.summary: HB 7063 (2023) provides 20-year sales tax exemptions for qualifying data centers. Actively pro-investment.

16.ai_policy: No comprehensive state AI law as of 2024
16.notes: Idaho Power hydroelectric makes this a prime renewable energy data center state.
16.name: Idaho
16.summary: IC §63-3622O exempts data center equipment from sales tax. Major Meta campus in Ada County (Eagle).

21.ai_policy: Kentucky AI Act (HB 4, 2023) — limited scope, state agency AI procurement guidelines
21.notes: Kentucky's low power costs and central US location drive growing data center investment.
21.name: Kentucky
21.summary: KRS §139.517 provides sales and use tax exemption for qualifying data centers. Louisville is a growing hub.

29.data_center_law: Missouri Chapters 353 and 100 (property tax abatement programs)
29.ai_policy: Missouri Executive Order on AI (2023) — state agency AI use guidance
29.notes: Missouri lacks a specific data center sales tax exemption law but uses Chapter 353 abatements.
29.name: Missouri
29.summary: Chapter 353 property tax abatement available. No specific data center sales tax exemption law.

35.ai_policy: NM AI in State Government Task Force (2024) — studying AI use in state agencies
35.notes: New Mexico's renewable energy resources and tax incentives attract hyperscale operators.
35.name: New Mexico
35.summary: NMSA §7-9-57 provides GRT deduction for qualifying data centers. Meta and Microsoft have invested in Albuquerque.

40.ai_policy: Oklahoma AI Act (HB 3939, 2024) — AI transparency requirements for state agencies
40.notes: Oklahoma's full sales and use tax exemption has driven significant Microsoft investment.
40.name: Oklahoma
40.summary: 68 O.S. §1359.2 provides full sales and use tax exemption for qualifying data centers. Microsoft has announced multi-billion dollar investments in OKC.

01.name: Alabama
01.summary: Alabama is a strongly favorable data center investment destination, anchored by Meta's Huntsville campus. The Alabama Jobs Act (§40-18-376) provides income tax credits and sales tax abatements for qualifying capital investments including data centers. The state's competitive power rates via TVA and available land make it attractive for hyperscale development.

sources.label: Alabama Jobs Act — Made in Alabama

24.name: Maryland
24.summary: Maryland hosts a significant data center cluster in the DC suburbs, including major campuses in Montgomery and Prince George's counties. The state offers property tax exemptions for qualifying data center equipment. Some county-level concerns exist around power grid impacts and land use in the I-270 technology corridor, but no statewide restrictions are enacted.

sources.label: Maryland Department of Assessments and Taxation — Data Center Credit

34.name: New Jersey
34.summary: Governor Mikie Sherrill signed the Data Center Fair Share Act on July 7, 2026, making New Jersey the first state to create a separate ratepayer class for large data centers. The law requires facilities using 50 MW or more of power to commit to paying at least 85% of projected electricity costs for a decade. It creates a first-of-its-kind program incentivizing data centers to secure clean energy capacity by reducing demand elsewhere on the grid — data centers can pay for households to replace energy-inefficient appliances with heat pumps or install rooftop solar/batteries, receiving priority in the interconnection queue in return. When the grid is strained, data centers must curtail before residential ratepayers are impacted. Sponsored by Senator John Burzichelli. New Jersey hosts a significant colocation data center cluster in Parsippany, Piscataway, and Secaucus.

sources.label: NJ Governor Sherrill — Signs legislation on energy including Data Center Fair Share Act (official)
NJ Assembly Democrats — Data Center Fair Share Act signed into law
NJ1015: New Jersey becomes first state to put AI data centers in their own ratepayer class

38.name: North Dakota
38.summary: North Dakota is a favorable data center investment destination with low power costs, cold climate (reducing cooling overhead), and a 5-year property tax exemption for qualifying data center equipment under NDCC §57-02-08.31. Microsoft operates a major hyperscale campus in the state. The state government has actively marketed data center incentives.

sources.label: North Dakota NDCC §57-02-08.31 — Data Center Exemption

42.name: Pennsylvania
42.summary: Pennsylvania has a growing data center presence in the Philadelphia suburbs, Pittsburgh, and Lehigh Valley. Act 84 of 2016 created a sales tax exemption for qualifying data center equipment. Some county-level concerns exist around power use and suburban land use, but no statewide restrictions are enacted. PECO and PPL territories offer data center rate structures.

sources.label: Pennsylvania Act 84 of 2016 — Data Center Sales Tax Exemption

05.name: Arkansas
05.summary: Arkansas is a favorable data center investment destination with a sales and use tax exemption for qualifying data center equipment (§26-52-465) and the ADVANTAGE Arkansas income tax incentive program. Little Rock and Northwest Arkansas have attracted data center investment. Competitive power rates from Entergy Arkansas and a low-regulation environment make the state attractive for edge and enterprise facilities.

sources.label: Arkansas DFA — Data Center Tax Exemption

10.name: Delaware
10.summary: Delaware hosts a moderate data center cluster in New Castle County (Wilmington area), benefiting from proximity to the Philadelphia–Baltimore–Washington corridor. No Delaware-specific data center tax incentive exists, but the state's favorable corporate tax structure and existing financial services data center ecosystem support the market. No statewide data center restrictions are enacted.

sources.label: Delaware Division of Corporations

20.name: Kansas
20.summary: Kansas has a moderate data center presence primarily in the Kansas City metro (Johnson County) and Wichita areas. The state's IMPACT program provides payroll withholding tax rebates for qualifying capital projects. No Kansas-specific data center sales tax exemption exists, though the state's low cost of living and power rates attract smaller-scale facilities. No statewide data center restrictions are enacted.

sources.label: Kansas Commerce — IMPACT Program

46.name: South Dakota
46.summary: South Dakota is a favorable data center investment destination with zero corporate income tax, competitive electrical rates, and a business-friendly regulatory environment. Sioux Falls (Minnehaha County) is the primary data center market. T-Mobile and regional carriers operate significant facilities. No statewide data center restrictions or specific tax incentive programs exist — the competitive advantage is the absence of corporate taxation.

sources.label: South Dakota GOED — Business Climate

49.name: Utah
49.summary: Utah is a fast-growing data center market anchored in Salt Lake County. The state's data center sales tax exemption (Utah Code Ann. §59-12-104(77)) and EDTIF incentive program support hyperscale investment. Google, C7 Data Centers, Novva, and regional colocation operators have significant facilities. Rocky Mountain Power provides reliable power; fiber infrastructure via the I-15 corridor is well-developed.

sources.label: Utah Code Ann. §59-12-104(77) — Data Center Exemption

23.name: Maine
23.summary: Maine has no statewide AI-specific or data center regulation as of 2026. The Act to Establish the Maine Artificial Intelligence Advisory Council (LD 2064, enacted 2024) creates an advisory body to study AI governance but does not impose mandates. The Maine PUC has flagged data center load growth as a grid-planning concern. Portland (Cumberland County) adopted a study resolution on data center siting impacts. Maine's renewable portfolio standard (35% by 2025, 80% by 2030) applies to large load customers including data centers through CMP tariff schedules.

sources.label: Maine LD 2064 (2024) — AI Advisory Council Act
Maine PUC — Transmission Interconnection Planning

30.name: Montana
30.summary: Montana has no statewide data center regulation or AI-specific legislation as of 2026. Data center siting is managed at the county level under local zoning authority. Montana's abundant hydroelectric power and cold climate support free-cooling designs, but grid connectivity to major fiber routes is limited outside Missoula and Billings. Montana has no general sales tax, so data center equipment purchases are exempt by default. The 2025 Legislature considered but did not advance a dedicated data center tax credit bill.

sources.label: Montana DNRC — Water Rights Administration
Montana Department of Commerce — Business Resources

33.name: New Hampshire
33.summary: New Hampshire has no statewide AI-specific regulation or data center siting legislation as of 2026. The state's unique tax profile — no general sales tax, no income tax — makes it one of the most favorable states for data center equipment procurement. The NH PUC regulates Eversource and Unitil as electric utilities but has no specific data center provisions. NH's 2025 legislative session considered but did not advance an AI transparency bill. Site Assessment of Market Power (SAMP) proceedings at the PUC may affect large load interconnection in Hillsborough and Rockingham counties.

sources.label: NH Division of Economic Development
NH PUC — Electric Utility Regulation

50.name: Vermont
50.summary: Vermont has no statewide AI-specific regulation or data center legislation as of 2026. The state's Act 174 (2016) Energy Planning framework and Renewable Energy Standard (75% by 2032, 100% by 2035 under 30 V.S.A. §8005) apply to all large load customers including data centers through Green Mountain Power and VELCO. Vermont's Public Utility Commission reviews Certificate of Public Good applications for new large electrical loads. Vermont has no data center-specific tax incentive but the clean energy grid profile is a competitive differentiator. The 2024 Vermont Legislature considered and tabled an AI Impact Assessment bill.

sources.label: Vermont Agency of Commerce — Economic Development
Vermont PUC — Certificate of Public Good

54.name: West Virginia
54.summary: West Virginia enacted the EDGE Act (HB 2002, 2023) creating a 25% investment tax credit for qualified data center equipment and construction costs, the first dedicated data center incentive in the state. Appalachian Power (AEP) and Appalachian Electric Power provide baseload capacity at among the lowest industrial rates in the eastern US. The Governor's office has designated data center attraction as an economic development priority under the WV Forward strategic plan. No statewide AI-specific regulation has been enacted. The WV Legislature considered an AI transparency bill in 2025 but did not advance it.

sources.label: WV EDGE Act (HB 2002, 2023)
WV Department of Economic Development

28.name: Mississippi
28.summary: Mississippi has no statewide AI-specific regulation or dedicated data center legislation as of 2026. The Mississippi Development Authority (MDA) promotes data center investment under the state's general economic development programs, including the Advantage Jobs Rebate (which can apply to technology companies) and sales tax exemptions for manufacturing equipment that may extend to qualifying data center machinery. Mississippi Power (Southern Company) and Entergy Mississippi provide baseload power at competitive industrial rates. The state's low cost of living, available land, and natural gas generation resources attract energy-intensive workloads. No AI governance bill has been introduced in the Mississippi Legislature as of 2025.

sources.label: Mississippi Development Authority
Mississippi Power — Industrial Rates

02.name: Alaska
02.summary: Alaska has no statewide AI-specific regulation or dedicated data center legislation as of 2026. The state's remote geography and high utility costs (diesel power in many areas) limit commercial data center development. GCI (General Communication Inc.) and Alaska Communications maintain telecom infrastructure and small data center assets in Anchorage and Fairbanks. The Trans-Alaska Pipeline System (TAPS) and North Slope oil operations generate industrial IT and edge computing demand. Alaska has no personal income tax and no state sales tax, making equipment acquisition favorable for the limited commercial data center market. The Federal government (DoD, Coast Guard, NOAA) is the primary driver of data center investment in the state.

sources.label: Alaska Dept. of Commerce — Business Programs
GCI — Alaska Network Infrastructure

15.name: Hawaii
15.summary: Hawaii has no statewide AI-specific regulation or dedicated data center legislation as of 2026. The state's high electricity costs (among the highest in the US due to oil-fired generation, though rapidly transitioning to renewables) make conventional data center operations expensive. Hawaiian Telcom (Consolidated Communications) and Lumen/CenturyLink maintain carrier-grade network data center infrastructure. Pacific subsea cable landing sites in Oahu (including the SEA-US and TGN-IA cables) create strategic interconnection value for trans-Pacific workloads. Hawaii's 100% renewable portfolio standard by 2045 (HRS §269-92) is reshaping the power cost structure for large loads. No AI governance bill has been enacted as of 2025.

sources.label: Hawaii PUC — Energy Regulation
Hawaiian Telcom — Data Center Services

11.name: District of Columbia
11.summary: Washington, DC is a significant government and commercial data center market anchored by federal agency cloud contracts and carrier-neutral facilities serving the federal and Mid-Atlantic markets. The DC Department of Energy & Environment enforces the Building Energy Performance Standards (BEPS) Act (DC Law 22-257, 2019), which requires large buildings including data centers to meet energy performance benchmarks or pay compliance fees — one of the most stringent commercial building energy laws in the US. The DC Council enacted the DC AI Accountability Act (B25-0644, introduced 2024) establishing requirements for District government use of automated decision systems, including bias audits, algorithmic impact assessments, and public transparency disclosures. DC has no data center-specific sales or property tax exemption, though the District's general business tax structure and federal procurement market are competitive drivers. Major colocation and carrier-hotel facilities serve the federal agency market. The bulk of the DC region's hyperscale data center capacity is located across the Potomac in Northern Virginia (Loudoun, Prince William, and Fairfax counties).

sources.label: DC DOEE — Building Energy Performance Standards (BEPS)
DC B25-0644 — AI Accountability Act
DC Office of Unified Communications — Technology Infrastructure
